summary: DeVault argues that jQuery is unnecessary for modern web development, demonstrating that native browser APIs can handle the same tasks jQuery is commonly used for. He walks through common jQuery use cases — DOM querying, Ajax requests, animations, and tree traversal — showing vanilla JavaScript equivalents for each. While the native code is more verbose, he argues it's simpler than most developers expect. He uses his own open-source project MediaCrush as proof that sophisticated web applications can be built without jQuery. The post concludes that developers should skip jQuery for new projects and rely on MDN documentation instead.
key_points: Native browser APIs like querySelectorAll, classList, and XMLHttpRequest cover most jQuery use cases with reasonable verbosity
CSS animations should replace JavaScript-based animations, making jQuery's animate() unnecessary
The verbosity gap between jQuery and vanilla JS is smaller than most developers assume
jQuery adds an unnecessary 91kb dependency and extra HTTP request when browsers already provide the needed functionality
Dropping legacy browser support for cosmetic features like animations is an acceptable tradeoff
money_quotes: It's true. You really don't need jQuery. Modern web browsers can do most of what you want from jQuery, without jQuery.
I feel like adding jQuery is one of the first things a web developer does to their shiny new website. It just isn't really necessary in this day and age.
Also a bit more verbose than jQuery, but much simpler than you might've expected.
Without jQuery, I wouldn't do this in Javascript. I'd use the magic of CSS animations.
The main advantage of jQuery is a less verbose syntax.
That extra request, 91kb, and load time are probably negligible, but it's still a little less clean than it could be.
There's no need to go back and rid all of your projects of jQuery, but I'd suggest that for your next one, you try to do without.
key_insight: jQuery's convenience comes at an unnecessary cost when modern browser APIs already provide the same capabilities with only slightly more verbose syntax.
